questions.question:
Match the following:

P. Prim's algorithm for minimum spanning tree          i.   Backtracking
Q. Floyd-Warshall algorithm for all pairs shortest path  ii.  Greedy method
R. Merge sort                                           iii. Dynamic programming
S. Hamiltonian circuit                                  iv.  Divide and conquer

A. P-ii, Q-iii, R-iv, S-i
B. P-i, Q-iii, R-iv, S-ii
C. P-ii, Q-iii, R-iv, S-i
D. P-ii, Q-iii, R-i, S-iv
questions.to_find: Correct matching of well-known algorithms to their design paradigms.

understand.plain: Four classic algorithms are listed on the left and four algorithm design paradigms on the right. We need to identify which paradigm each algorithm belongs to. Prim's builds an MST greedily, Floyd-Warshall uses DP for all-pairs shortest paths, Merge sort splits and merges (divide and conquer), and Hamiltonian circuit is solved by trying all possibilities (backtracking).

keywords.explain: Makes the locally optimal choice at each step hoping to reach global optimum. Does not reconsider past choices.
keywords.example: Prim's MST always picks the minimum weight edge that extends the current tree.

keywords.explain: Solves problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems, storing results to avoid recomputation.
keywords.example: Floyd-Warshall fills a 2D table of shortest distances through intermediate vertices.

keywords.explain: Recursively splits the problem into independent subproblems, solves them, and combines the results.
keywords.example: Merge sort splits array in half, sorts each half, then merges the sorted halves.

keywords.explain: Explores all candidate solutions, abandoning ('backtracking') a partial solution as soon as it is known to be invalid.
keywords.example: Hamiltonian circuit tries each vertex extension and backtracks when no valid extension exists.

understand.visual_alt: Two columns: Algorithms (Prim's MST, Floyd-Warshall, Merge sort, Hamiltonian circuit) and Paradigms (Backtracking, Greedy, Dynamic Programming, Divide and Conquer). Arrows show the correct mapping: P->ii, Q->iii, R->iv, S->i.

given.aim: Match each algorithm to the algorithm design paradigm it uses.

terms.meaning: Algorithm that grows a minimum spanning tree by greedily adding the cheapest edge connecting the tree to a new vertex.
terms.example: At each step pick the cheapest edge from the frontier.
terms.connects: Greedy choice at every step → Greedy method (ii).

terms.meaning: Computes shortest paths between all pairs of vertices using a DP recurrence over intermediate vertices.
terms.example: d[i][j] = min(d[i][j], d[i][k]+d[k][j]) for each k.
terms.connects: Stores and reuses subproblem solutions → Dynamic programming (iii).

terms.meaning: Sorting algorithm that recursively divides the array into halves, sorts each, then merges.
terms.example: Sort [3,1,4,2] by splitting into [3,1] and [4,2], sorting, then merging.
terms.connects: Divide into independent subproblems and combine → Divide and conquer (iv).

terms.meaning: A cycle that visits every vertex exactly once; finding one requires exploring all possibilities and pruning dead ends.
terms.example: Try adding each unvisited vertex, backtrack if stuck.

given.plan: Identify the core mechanism each algorithm uses: Prim's makes greedy local choices, Floyd-Warshall stores subproblem results in a table, Merge sort recursively divides and merges, and Hamiltonian circuit tries all extensions and backtracks.

steps.title: Classify each algorithm
steps.apply:
P (Prim's MST): always picks minimum-weight edge extending the tree — Greedy (ii).
Q (Floyd-Warshall): builds solution table using d[i][k]+d[k][j] recurrence — Dynamic programming (iii).
R (Merge sort): splits array into halves recursively, then merges — Divide and conquer (iv).
S (Hamiltonian circuit): tries each possible next vertex and backtracks on dead ends — Backtracking (i).
steps.note: These are canonical examples of each paradigm and frequently tested in GATE.

steps.title: Form the matching and select option
steps.apply:
Matching: P-ii, Q-iii, R-iv, S-i.
Comparing with options: Option A states exactly P-ii, Q-iii, R-iv, S-i.
steps.note: Option D incorrectly maps Hamiltonian to DP and Merge sort to Backtracking — both wrong.

solution.result: Option A: P-ii, Q-iii, R-iv, S-i is correct.

questions.formula_note: Classification of canonical algorithms by design paradigm: Greedy (Prim's), Dynamic Programming (Floyd-Warshall), Divide and Conquer (Merge sort), Backtracking (Hamiltonian circuit).

questions.question:
Given below are some algorithms, and some algorithm design paradigms.

1. Dijkstra's Shortest Path
2. Floyd-Warshall algorithm to compute all pair shortest path
3. Binary search on a sorted array
4. Backtracking search on a graph

Design Paradigms:
i.   Divide and Conquer
ii.  Dynamic Programming
iii. Greedy design
iv.  Depth-first search
v.   Breadth-first search

Match the above algorithms on the left to the corresponding design paradigm they follow.

A. 1-i, 2-iii, 3-i, 4-v
B. 1-iii, 2-iii, 3-i, 4-v
C. 1-iii, 2-ii, 3-i, 4-iv
D. 1-iii, 2-ii, 3-i, 4-v
questions.to_find: Match each algorithm to its algorithm design paradigm.

understand.plain: Four well-known algorithms are listed along with five design paradigms. We need to figure out which paradigm best describes each algorithm. Dijkstra's uses a greedy approach, Floyd-Warshall uses dynamic programming, binary search repeatedly halves the search space (divide and conquer), and backtracking search is inherently depth-first.

keywords.explain: Greedy single-source shortest path algorithm that always relaxes the nearest unvisited vertex.
keywords.example: Pick vertex with smallest tentative distance, update its neighbors, repeat.

keywords.explain: Explores solution space by going deep (like DFS), and when a dead end is reached, it backs up to try another path.
keywords.example: N-Queens: place queen, go deeper, if conflict backtrack to previous row.

Dijkstra's Shortest Path</text><text x="25" y="90" class="sub ink">2. Floyd-Warshall (APSP)</text><text x="25" y="120" class="sub ink">3. Binary search</text><text x="25" y="150" class="sub ink">4. Backtracking search</text><text x="315" y="60" class="sub ink">i.   Divide and Conquer</text><text x="315" y="90" class="sub ink">ii.  Dynamic Programming</text><text x="315" y="120" class="sub ink">iii. Greedy design</text><text x="315" y="150" class="sub ink">iv.  Depth-first search</text><text x="315" y="175" class="sub ink">v. understand.visual_alt: Two columns listing algorithms (Dijkstra's, Floyd-Warshall, Binary search, Backtracking) and paradigms (Divide and Conquer, Dynamic Programming, Greedy, DFS, BFS). Correct mapping: 1-iii, 2-ii, 3-i, 4-iv.

given.aim: Identify the design paradigm that each of the four algorithms fundamentally uses.

terms.meaning: Single-source shortest path algorithm using a priority queue; at each step greedily selects the unvisited vertex with the smallest current distance.
terms.example: Starting from s, always extend to the closest known vertex.
terms.connects: Greedy choice at every iteration → Greedy design (iii).

terms.meaning: All-pairs shortest path algorithm; uses the recurrence d[i][j] = min(d[i][j], d[i][k]+d[k][j]) storing subproblem results.
terms.example: Fills an n x n table iteratively over k = 1..n.
terms.connects: Overlapping subproblems + optimal substructure → Dynamic Programming (ii).

terms.meaning: Searches a sorted array by comparing the middle element, then recursively searching the left or right half.
terms.example: Find 7 in [1,3,5,7,9]: compare with 5, then search right half [7,9].
terms.connects: Splits problem in half each time → Divide and Conquer (i).

terms.meaning: Explores graph by going as deep as possible along a path, then retreating when a dead end or constraint violation is found.
terms.example: Finding a Hamiltonian path: explore depth-first, backtrack on revisiting a vertex.
terms.connects: Depth-first exploration of solution space → Depth-first search (iv).

given.plan: Determine the core strategy each algorithm applies: Dijkstra's greedily picks the nearest vertex (Greedy), Floyd-Warshall fills a DP table (DP), Binary search halves the problem (D&C), and Backtracking is inherently a depth-first traversal of the solution tree.

steps.title: Classify each algorithm
steps.apply:
1. Dijkstra's: greedy selection via min-priority queue → iii (Greedy design).
2. Floyd-Warshall: DP recurrence d[i][j] = min(d[i][j], d[i][k]+d[k][j]) → ii (Dynamic Programming).
3. Binary search: halve search space at each step → i (Divide and Conquer).
4. Backtracking search: DFS through solution tree, backtrack on failure → iv (Depth-first search).
steps.note: Backtracking is NOT Breadth-first search; BFS explores level by level, while backtracking explores depth-first.

steps.title: Select the matching option
steps.apply:
Mapping is 1-iii, 2-ii, 3-i, 4-iv.
Option C: 1-iii, 2-ii, 3-i, 4-iv — matches exactly.
Option D has 4-v (BFS) which is incorrect.
steps.note: The distinction between option C and D is only in algorithm 4: backtracking is DFS (iv), not BFS (v).

questions.formula_note: Greedy (Dijkstra's), Dynamic Programming (Floyd-Warshall recurrence), Divide and Conquer (Binary search halving), Depth-first search (Backtracking).

questions.question:
Consider the following table:

Algorithms          Design Paradigms
(P) Kruskal         (I)  Divide and Conquer
(Q) Quicksort       (II) Greedy
(R) Floyd-Warshall  (III) Dynamic Programming

Match the algorithms to the design paradigms they are based on.

A. (P) -> (II), (Q) -> (III), (R) -> (I)
B. (P) -> (III), (Q) -> (I), (R) -> (II)
C. (P) -> (II), (Q) -> (I), (R) -> (III)
D. (P) -> (I), (Q) -> (II), (R) -> (III)
questions.to_find: Match Kruskal, Quicksort, and Floyd-Warshall to their respective design paradigms.

understand.plain: Three famous algorithms are listed with three design paradigms. Kruskal's algorithm builds an MST by always choosing the globally cheapest edge not forming a cycle — a greedy approach. Quicksort partitions the array and recursively sorts each part — divide and conquer. Floyd-Warshall computes all-pairs shortest paths using a DP recurrence — dynamic programming.

keywords.explain: MST algorithm that sorts all edges and adds the cheapest one that doesn't create a cycle, using Union-Find.
keywords.example: Edges sorted: (A,B,1), (B,C,2), (A,C,3) — pick (A,B,1), (B,C,2), skip (A,C,3) since cycle.

keywords.explain: Sorting algorithm that picks a pivot, partitions the array so elements less than pivot come first, then recursively sorts each partition.
keywords.example: Array [3,6,1,8,2,9]: pivot=3, partition to [1,2,3,6,8,9], recurse on [1,2] and [6,8,9].

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 520 180" width="520" height="180"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="0" y="0" width="520" height="180" rx="8" fill="var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)"/><rect x="10" y="10" width="180" height="155" rx="6" class="bg"/><rect x="230" y="10" width="280" height="155" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="100" y="35" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">Algorithm</text><text x="370" y="35" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">Paradigm</text><text x="25" y="68" class="sub ink">(P) Kruskal</text><text x="25" y="103" class="sub ink">(Q) Quicksort</text><text x="25" y="138" class="sub ink">(R) Floyd-Warshall</text><text x="245" y="68" class="sub ink">(I)   Divide and Conquer</text><text x="245" y="103" class="sub ink">(II)  Greedy</text><text x="245" y="138" class="sub ink">(III) Dynamic Programming</text><line x1="195" y1="65" x2="228" y2="100" stroke="var(--ok,#1D9E75)" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="195" y1="100" x2="228" y2="65" stroke="var(--ok,#1D9E75)" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="195" y1="135" x2="228" y2="135" stroke="var(--ok,#1D9E75)" stroke-width="2"/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table with three algorithms (Kruskal, Quicksort, Floyd-Warshall) and three paradigms (Divide and Conquer, Greedy, Dynamic Programming). Correct mapping: Kruskal->Greedy, Quicksort->Divide and Conquer, Floyd-Warshall->Dynamic Programming.

given.aim: Match Kruskal's, Quicksort, and Floyd-Warshall to their algorithm design paradigms among Divide and Conquer, Greedy, and Dynamic Programming.

terms.meaning: Builds MST by greedily selecting the minimum weight edge that doesn't form a cycle, using a Union-Find structure.
terms.example: Sort edges by weight, pick cheapest edge (u,v) where u and v are in different components.
terms.connects: Greedy selection at each step → Paradigm (II) Greedy.

terms.meaning: Sorting algorithm that partitions the array around a pivot and recursively sorts each partition independently.
terms.connects: Splits into independent subproblems and conquers each → Paradigm (I) Divide and Conquer.

terms.meaning: Computes shortest paths between all pairs of vertices by building up solutions through successively including each vertex as an intermediate.
terms.connects: Stores and reuses subproblem results → Paradigm (III) Dynamic Programming.

given.plan: Recognize the core mechanism: Kruskal's makes a greedy edge-selection, Quicksort divides the array and conquers each half independently, Floyd-Warshall fills a DP table. Map each to its paradigm and select the matching option.

steps.title: Identify paradigm for each algorithm
steps.apply:
(P) Kruskal's MST: sort edges, pick globally cheapest safe edge each time — no subproblem table, pure greedy → (II) Greedy.
(Q) Quicksort: partition around pivot, then recursively sort left and right partitions independently → (I) Divide and Conquer.
(R) Floyd-Warshall: d[i][j] = min over intermediate vertex k; stores all pairwise distances in a table → (III) Dynamic Programming.
steps.note: Quicksort is not DP because the subproblems are independent (no overlap) and nothing is memoized.

steps.title: Identify the correct option
steps.apply:
Mapping: P -> II, Q -> I, R -> III.
Option C: (P) -> (II), (Q) -> (I), (R) -> (III) — matches exactly.
Option D incorrectly maps Kruskal to D&C and Quicksort to Greedy.
steps.note: A common mistake is confusing Quicksort with DP or confusing Kruskal with D&C; both errors appear in the wrong options.

solution.result: Option C: (P) -> (II) Greedy, (Q) -> (I) Divide and Conquer, (R) -> (III) Dynamic Programming.

questions.formula_note: Kruskal's uses Greedy edge selection (Union-Find), Quicksort uses Divide and Conquer (partition-recurse), Floyd-Warshall uses Dynamic Programming (d[i][j][k] recurrence).
